title: Digital Rights Ireland
parties: Digital Rights Ireland Ltd v Minister for Communications, Marine and Natural Resources and Others; Kärntner Landesregierung and Others
summary: The CJEU invalidated the Data Retention Directive 2006/24/EC as incompatible with Articles 7 and 8 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. The Court held that general and indiscriminate retention of traffic and location data constitutes a particularly serious interference with fundamental rights, establishing a proportionality benchmark that continues to shape GDPR enforcement and data minimization principles.

title: Tele2 Sverige
parties: Tele2 Sverige AB v Post- och telestyrelsen; Secretary of State for the Home Department v Tom Watson and Others
summary: Following Digital Rights Ireland, the CJEU confirmed that EU law precludes national legislation requiring general and indiscriminate retention of traffic and location data. The Court elaborated that targeted retention may be permissible where strictly necessary, establishing key principles on the proportionality of data processing that directly inform GDPR data minimization and purpose limitation.

title: Google LLC (Territorial Scope of De-referencing)
parties: Google LLC v Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL)
summary: The CJEU ruled that the right to de-referencing under EU law does not require a search engine operator to carry out de-referencing on all its domain name versions globally. However, it must dereference on all EU Member State versions and take measures to discourage EU-based users from accessing delisted links on non-EU domains. This case defined the geographic boundaries of the right to erasure.

title: Google (Accurate De-referencing)
parties: TU and RE v Google LLC
summary: The CJEU held that a search engine operator must dereference content displayed in search results, including thumbnail images, where the information is manifestly inaccurate. The data subject does not need to obtain a prior court order against the content publisher; they must merely demonstrate that the claim is manifestly unfounded. This strengthened the right to erasure in relation to search engine content presentation.

title: Nacionalinis visuomenės sveikatos centras
parties: Nacionalinis visuomenės sveikatos centras prie Sveikatos apsaugos ministerijos v Valstybinė duomenų apsaugos inspekcija
summary: The CJEU clarified that joint controllership can arise from participation in determining the purposes and means of processing, even without a formal agreement. It held that a DPA may fine a controller for processing carried out by a processor on its behalf. Crucially, the Court confirmed that fault (intentional or negligent conduct) is required for imposing administrative fines under Art. 83 GDPR.

title: Deutsche Wohnen
parties: Deutsche Wohnen SE v Staatsanwaltschaft Berlin
summary: The CJEU ruled that administrative fines under Art. 83 GDPR can be imposed directly on an undertaking (legal person) without needing to first identify a specific natural person who committed the infringement. However, fines require at least negligent conduct — strict liability is not permitted. This landmark ruling resolved a major uncertainty about corporate GDPR fine liability across the EU.

title: SCHUFA I (Scoring)
parties: SCHUFA Holding AG (Scoring)
summary: The CJEU held that the establishment of a probability score by a credit reference agency constitutes 'automated individual decision-making' under Art. 22 GDPR where that score is transmitted to a third party that relies heavily on it for its own decision. This broadened the scope of Art. 22 significantly, with major implications for AI-based and algorithmic decision-making across all sectors.

title: SCHUFA II (Data Retention from Public Registers)
parties: SCHUFA Holding AG (Retention of insolvency data)
summary: The CJEU ruled that a private credit agency's practice of storing personal insolvency data longer than the public insolvency register itself is unlawful under the GDPR, unless the controller can demonstrate the necessity for such extended storage. This established important limits on how long private entities may retain data originally sourced from public registers.

title: Österreichische Datenschutzbehörde (CRIF)
parties: Österreichische Datenschutzbehörde and CRIF GmbH
summary: The CJEU interpreted the right to obtain a 'copy' of personal data under Art. 15(3) GDPR as requiring a faithful and intelligible reproduction of all personal data undergoing processing. This means providing the data in a comprehensive format, including where necessary extracts from documents or databases, not merely a summary. This significantly expanded the practical scope of access requests.

title: Pankki S
parties: J.M. v Pankki S
summary: The CJEU held that the data subject's right of access under Art. 15 GDPR is not conditional on the data subject stating the reasons for the request. A controller cannot refuse access merely because the request is motivated by purposes unrelated to data protection. The right of access exists regardless of the purpose for which it is exercised, though manifestly unfounded or excessive requests may be refused under Art. 12(5).

title: juris GmbH
parties: GP v juris GmbH
summary: The CJEU addressed the right of access to personal data appearing in a legal database's published court decisions. It confirmed that the right of access under Art. 15 GDPR applies even where personal data appears in publicly available documents, though practical limitations on the scope of compliance may apply.

title: Schrems III (Facebook Meta)
parties: Maximillian Schrems v Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd
summary: The CJEU ruled that the principle of data minimization under Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR precludes a social network platform from using all personal data obtained for targeted advertising purposes without restriction as to time. The Court also held that where a data subject publicly reveals their sexual orientation, this does not authorize processing of other data concerning sexual orientation via automated means for targeted advertising.

title: Vyriausioji tarnybinės etikos komisija
parties: OT v Vyriausioji tarnybinės etikos komisija
summary: The CJEU held that national legislation requiring online publication of personal data from declarations of interest for anti-corruption purposes must comply with the proportionality principle under GDPR. The online publication of all such data, including sensitive data about cohabitants, was disproportionate. This case established important balancing criteria between public interest transparency and data protection.

title: Valsts ieņēmumu dienests (SIA SS)
parties: SIA 'SS' v Valsts ieņēmumu dienests
summary: The CJEU ruled that a tax authority's request for bulk disclosure of personal data from an online advertising platform must comply with the data minimization principle. Requiring transmission of all data about all advertisements without any criteria related to the tax inquiry objective is disproportionate. The decision set important limits on public authority data requests.

title: Jehovan todistajat (Jehovah's Witnesses)
parties: Tietosuojavaltuutettu v Jehovan todistajat
summary: The CJEU held that the Jehovah's Witnesses community acts as a data controller for notes collected by its members during door-to-door preaching, even without accessing the data itself. The household exemption does not apply. This significantly broadened the concept of 'controller' and confirmed that organised collection of personal data outside the purely personal sphere falls under data protection law.

title: Facebook Ireland (Belgian DPA)
parties: Facebook Ireland Ltd, Facebook Inc., Facebook Belgium BVBA v Gegevensbeschermingsautoriteit
summary: The CJEU clarified the one-stop-shop mechanism under GDPR, holding that a non-lead supervisory authority may exercise its power to bring proceedings before a national court for cross-border processing in specific circumstances defined in Art. 56(2) and Art. 66 GDPR. This case resolved critical questions about the allocation of DPA powers in cross-border cases.

title: Österreichische Datenschutzbehörde (Linz Municipality)
parties: Österreichische Datenschutzbehörde v Gemeinde Linz
summary: The CJEU addressed the DPO conflict of interest prohibition under Art. 38(6) GDPR, holding that a person who simultaneously determines the purposes and means of processing cannot serve as DPO. It also confirmed that Member States may exclude public authorities from administrative fines under Art. 83(7) but must still ensure effective enforcement.

title: Krankenversicherung Nordrhein
parties: ZQ v Medizinischer Dienst der Krankenversicherung Nordrhein
summary: The CJEU examined the processing of health data under Art. 9(2)(h) GDPR in the employment context. The Court held that where a medical service processes an employee's health data for occupational health assessment, this must comply with the specific safeguards for health data processing. Unauthorized access to health data by personnel without medical confidentiality obligations constitutes a GDPR violation.

title: IAB Europe (TCF)
parties: IAB Europe v Gegevensbeschermingsautoriteit
summary: The CJEU ruled that the TC String used in IAB Europe's Transparency and Consent Framework constitutes personal data as it can be linked to identifiable users. IAB Europe qualifies as a joint controller with participating organizations, even though it does not itself have direct access to the raw personal data. This decision has massive implications for the entire programmatic advertising ecosystem and consent management platforms.

title: Asociación Estatal de Entidades de Servicios de Atención a Domicilio (ADAD)
parties: Asociación Estatal de Entidades de Servicios de Atención a Domicilio v Diputación Foral de Gipuzkoa
summary: The CJEU assessed whether GDPR precludes national legislation requiring public procurement tenderers to disclose employee identity data. It held that while transparency in public procurement may justify data processing, the principle of proportionality requires that only data necessary for the legitimate objective be disclosed, reinforcing GDPR data minimization even in public contract contexts.

title: Norra Stockholm Bygg (Disclosure in Litigation)
parties: Norra Stockholm Bygg AB and Others v Per Nycander AB
summary: The CJEU held that GDPR must be taken into account when a national court orders disclosure of documents containing personal data in civil proceedings. Courts must balance the right to effective judicial protection against data protection rights, applying data minimization to limit disclosure to what is strictly necessary. This case clarified the intersection between civil procedure and GDPR.

title: Hauptpersonalrat der Lehrerinnen und Lehrer
parties: Hauptpersonalrat der Lehrerinnen und Lehrer beim Hessischen Kultusministerium
summary: The CJEU addressed whether teacher consent is required for live-streamed video lessons. The Court held that processing of teachers' personal data through video conferencing for educational purposes during COVID could be based on Art. 6(1)(e) (public interest), without requiring individual consent, provided adequate safeguards are in place.

title: Meta Platforms Ireland (Facebook Fan Pages - Sequel)
parties: Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd v Bundesverband der Verbraucherzentralen und Verbraucherverbände
summary: The CJEU held that consumer protection associations may bring representative actions against data protection infringements under Art. 80(2) GDPR, even without a specific mandate from a data subject and irrespective of actual infringement of specific data subjects' rights. This landmark ruling enabled collective GDPR enforcement by consumer organizations across the EU.

title: Koalitsia Demokratichna Bulgaria (Bulgarian Elections)
parties: Koalitsia Demokratichna Bulgaria - Obedinenie v Predsedatel na Narodnoto sabranie na Republika Bulgaria
summary: The CJEU examined the processing of personal identification numbers and biometric data in the context of electronic voting systems. It held that while ensuring electoral integrity is a legitimate public interest objective, the processing of such sensitive data must comply with strict GDPR safeguards and proportionality, including specific national legislation meeting Art. 9(2)(g) requirements.

title: Mousse (Right to Compensation and Controller Identification)
parties: Mousse v Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL) and Collecte Localisation Satellites (CLS)
summary: The CJEU clarified the relationship between the data subject's right to lodge a complaint with a DPA (Art. 77), the right to judicial remedy against a DPA (Art. 78), and the right to compensation (Art. 82). It confirmed that these remedies are complementary and can be exercised in parallel, and that a DPA's decision not to act on a complaint does not preclude a separate compensation claim.

title: Médecins du Monde
parties: Médecins du monde - Dokters van de Wereld ASBL v Commissaire général aux réfugiés et aux apatrides
summary: The CJEU addressed GDPR implications of processing biometric and health data in the asylum/refugee context. The Court confirmed that even in public interest contexts like migration management, processing of special category data must strictly comply with Art. 9(2) conditions and proportionality requirements, with particular attention to the vulnerable status of data subjects.

title: Gemeinde Ummendorf
parties: Gemeinde Ummendorf v Land Baden-Württemberg
summary: The CJEU addressed when public authorities must designate a DPO under Art. 37(1)(a) GDPR. The Court provided guidance on the obligation for all public authorities, including small municipalities, to appoint a DPO, and clarified that Member States may set additional criteria under Art. 37(4) for mandatory DPO appointment.

title: Gemeinde Ummendorf (Damages Quantum)
parties: Gemeinde Ummendorf (Damages for Unlawful Processing)
summary: Building on the Österreichische Post damages line of cases, the CJEU provided further guidance on how national courts should assess the quantum of non-material damages under Art. 82 GDPR. The Court emphasized that while Member States retain procedural autonomy in determining damages amounts, the compensation must be full and effective, and courts cannot apply de minimis thresholds.

title: K GmbH (Employee Monitoring)
parties: K GmbH
summary: The CJEU examined the balancing test required under Art. 6(1)(f) for employee monitoring scenarios and the interplay with the right to object under Art. 21. The Court clarified that where an employee successfully exercises the right to object, the controller must cease processing and erase the data unless compelling legitimate grounds override the data subject's interests.

title: Belgian State (Right of Access Scope)
parties: Belgian State v Autorité de protection des données
summary: The CJEU clarified the distinction between the right of access under GDPR Art. 15 and general administrative transparency rights. The Court held that Art. 15 grants access specifically to personal data and information about processing activities, not a general right to obtain administrative documents, even if those documents contain the data subject's personal data.

title: Procura della Repubblica (Criminal Proceedings and GDPR)
parties: Procura della Repubblica presso il Tribunale di Bolzano
summary: The CJEU clarified the boundary between GDPR and the Law Enforcement Directive (LED) 2016/680 regarding data processing in criminal proceedings. The Court held that the GDPR exclusion in Art. 2(2)(d) applies only to processing by competent authorities for law enforcement purposes as defined in the LED, and that ancillary administrative processing may still fall under GDPR.

title: Endemol Shine (Reality TV)
parties: Endemol Shine Finland Oy
summary: The CJEU addressed GDPR consent requirements in the context of reality television production, including whether disclosure of health-related information by participants constitutes valid consent for broadcast processing of special category data. The Court clarified conditions for valid consent under Art. 7 in media contexts and the scope of the journalistic exemption under Art. 85.

title: Agentsia po vpisvaniyata (Agency for Registrations)
parties: Agentsia po vpisvaniyata
summary: The CJEU held that the principle of data minimization and data protection by design under GDPR applies to the disclosure of personal data in commercial/company registers. Even where EU or national law mandates publication, the data disclosed must be limited to what is necessary, and access mechanisms should incorporate proportionality safeguards.

title: Ligue des droits humains (PNR)
parties: Ligue des droits humains ASBL v Autorité belge de protection des données
summary: Following its landmark 2022 PNR ruling (C-817/19), the CJEU provided further guidance on the proportionality of passenger name record data processing systems. The Court confirmed that systematic processing of PNR data must be limited to what is strictly necessary and that retention periods must be proportionate, applying GDPR principles even to EU PNR Directive processing.
